g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Stacks, a fundamental linear data structure in computer science.
A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. The last element added is the first to be removed.
g.text: Operations performed only at one end called the TOP

g.data-narration: Imagine cafeteria plates stacked on each other. You can only take the top plate, and new plates are added on top. This perfectly illustrates LIFO.
Stacks can be implemented using arrays. We use a 'top' pointer to track the index of the topmost element. Top starts at -1 for empty stack.
The push operation adds an element to the top. First check if stack is full, then increment top and add the item. This is O(1) time complexity.
g.text: Algorithm: Check if full → Increment top → Add item
void push(struct Stack* s, int item) {

g.data-narration: The pop operation removes and returns the top element. First check if stack is empty (underflow), then return item and decrement top. Also O(1).
g.text: Algorithm: Check if empty → Return item → Decrement top

g.data-narration: peek() returns top element without removing it. isEmpty() checks if top equals -1. isFull() checks if top equals MAX-1. All are O(1) operations.
Let's trace stack operations step by step. Starting with empty stack, we push 10, 20, 30. Then pop twice, and push 40. Watch the top pointer!
Overflow occurs when pushing to a full stack. Underflow occurs when popping from an empty stack. Always check isFull/isEmpty before operations!
Function call stack is crucial in programming. When a function is called, its info is pushed. When it returns, it's popped. This manages execution flow.
g.text: When a function is called, its parameters, return address, and local variables are pushed onto the stack.

g.data-narration: Stacks check parentheses balance: push opening brackets, pop for closing ones. If stack empty at end, expression is balanced!
Stacks are used everywhere: undo/redo in editors, browser history, DFS traversal, expression evaluation, and backtracking algorithms.
Array-based stacks have fixed size with O(1) operations but may waste memory. Linked list stacks are dynamic but need extra memory for pointers.
g.text: Comparison: Array vs Linked List Implementation

g.data-narration: All stack operations run in constant time O(1). This is true for both array and linked list implementations. This efficiency makes stacks ideal for many algorithms.
For exams: Remember LIFO, check underflow/overflow, don't confuse peek with pop, practice tracing operations, and know the applications.
g.text: • LIFO is the core - last element pushed is first popped
• Always check isEmpty() before pop() to avoid underflow
• peek() views without removing; pop() removes - don't confuse!

g.data-narration: In summary, stacks follow LIFO with push, pop, and peek operations. They are implemented via arrays or linked lists, each with O(1) complexity. Essential for many algorithms!
